texts.full_text: analytic expression for the photoluminescence spectral function of a model polariton system, which describes a quantum dot, with a finite number of fermionic levels, strongly interacting with the lowest photon mode of a pillar microcavity. Energy eigenvalues and wavefunctions of the electron-hole-photon system are obtained by numerically diagonalizing the Hamiltonian. Pumping and photon losses through the cavity mirrors are described with a master equation, which is solved in order to determine the stationary density matrix. The photon first-order correlation function, from which the spectral function is found, is computed with the help of the Quantum Regression Theorem. The spectral function qualitatively describes the polariton lasing regime in the model, corresponding to pumping rates two orders of magnitude lower than those needed for ordinary (photon) lasing. The second-order coherence functions for the photon and the electron-hole subsystems are computed as functions of the pumping rate.' author: - 'Carlos Andrés Vera$^{(a)}$, Herbert Vinck-Posada$^{(a)}$, and Augusto González$^{(b)}$' title: | Polariton lasing in a multilevel quantum dot\ strongly coupled to a single photon mode --- Introduction {#sec1} ============ Excitonic polaritons are quasi-particles that result from the strong coupling between cavity photons and exciton excitations of semiconductor materials. In recent years, these hybrid light–matter entities have become promising candidates

The game just feels darker and more grounded in reality, which might be a bit of a departure from the more cartoonish, over-the-top feel of the first one. texts.full_text: planet. We express the required damping action to match the observations, and we show that the inner disk can play this role. This result applies for instance to the system GJ 876: we reproduce the observed orbital elements through a fully hydrodynamical simulation of the evolution of the resonant planets.' address: - 'Institut für Astronomie & Astrophysik – Universität Túbingen The formation and stability of resonant planet pairs in exoplanetary systems are intriguing topics that have garnered significant attention from astrophysicists. The study delves into the complex dynamics governing these interactions, particularly focusing on how planets migrate within their host protoplanetary discs. ### Introduction Resonance plays a crucial role in shaping the architecture of planetary systems around other stars. When an outer planet migrates towards its star at sufficient speed and with appropriate inclination relative to any inner planets or disk material, it can capture these bodies into resonant configurations where their orbital periods are related by simple ratios (e.g., 2:1). This resonance condition is not static; as the outer planet continues inward migration, gravitational interactions between the pair cause their eccentricities and inclinations to oscillate. ### Migration and Resonance Capture The migration of planets within protoplanetary discs typically occurs due to a combination of aerodynamic drag from the surrounding gas and torques exerted by any companion objects. As an outer planet migrates inward, it can encounter an inner planet or perturbations caused by disk material that are already at resonance with its own orbit. ### Eccentricity Growth The capture into Mean Motion Resonance (MMR) is often accompanied by a significant increase in eccentricities of the involved planets. This is due to gravitational torques from each other and their host star, which can lead to chaotic orbits if no damping mechanism acts upon them. ### Damping Mechanisms To prevent such excessive eccentricity growth, various mechanisms have been proposed that could dampen orbital oscillations in resonant systems: 1. Tidal Forces: The tidal forces exerted by the host star on its planets

texts.full_text: Community College (JSCC). JSCC offers a wide range of programs across various fields including business, computers & IT, healthcare professions such as nursing, medicine etc. To request course information or for more details regarding job listings: 1) Visit their official website. 2) Navigate to the "Admissions" section and find options related to 'Course Information' usually found under a tab like ‘Prospective Students’ or similar. You can also directly contact JSCC's Admission Office using phone, email address provided on college's webpage. Do note that due to recent changes in policies/management of the institution it is advisable you visit their official website for any updates and accurate information regarding job listings & course offerings as this will provide most current data. Finally remember each prospective student has unique needs when exploring educational options, JSCC encourages students considering career change or advancement through education seek guidance from college's Career Services Office. This office can offer personalized advice on choosing an appropriate program of study based upon individual goals a

texts.preview_tail: n conclusion, our research demonstrates that adipose tissue-derived stromal cells can significantly influence skin color by inhibiting melanogenesis through a mechanism, highlighting an important role
texts.full_text: dermal fibroblast or mixed cell type groups. Further studies revealed that adipose cells upregulated TGF-β1 (transforming growth factor beta 1) expression. The increased levels of TGF-β1 were found to directly inhibit melanocyte differentiation and pigmentation, as confirmed by in vitro studies using recombinant human TGF-β1 on primary cultures of epidermal cells. However, blocking the action of TGF-β1 with a neutralizing antibody or siRNA targeting TGF-β1 reversed these effects, leading to increased expression of MITF and other pigmentation-related genes. This suggests that the inhibitory effect of adipose tissue-derived stromal cells on melanocyte differentiation is mediated at least in part by levels of TGF-β1. These findings provide new insights into how different types of dermal stromal cells can modulate skin pigmentation and may have implications for understanding conditions associated with altered skin, such as vitiligo or hyperpigmentation disorders. Furthermore, this knowledge can be applied to develop novel therapeutic strategies aimed at regulating melanocyte activity in the treatment of various dermatological conditions. In conclusion, our research demonstrates that adipose tissue-derived stromal cells can significantly influence skin color by inhibiting melanogenesis through a mechanism, highlighting an important role
